summary: Full backend security audit against production preview URL. All 21 review-request checkpoints verified via 26 pytest cases (backend/tests/test_security_iter16.py) — 26 passed, 0 failed. PII is stripped from every public endpoint, rate limits fire on the exact off-by-one boundaries specified (login 6th, register 6th, ring-search 21st, contact 4th, bird-register 11th, discount-validate 21st), ownership and admin-only rules are enforced with correct HTTP codes, JWT cookies are HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite=None, blocked users get 403 Kontot är blockerat, Stripe webhook rejects missing/invalid signatures with 400, and regression flows (root, /contact happy path, /registered-birds → Stripe checkout URL) still work.

minor.issue: rate_limit runs before Swedish phone validation and before uniqueness check, so invalid submissions still consume the 10/h bucket. This is intentional (defence against submit-spam) but means a legitimate user who typos their phone 10× within the hour will be locked out. Consider bumping window to 15 min or raising limit.

rate_limit runs before code lookup; 20/min is fine but there is no per-code failure counter — an attacker can enumerate codes at 20/min per IP. Consider tightening to 10/min or adding an exponential backoff after N invalid codes.

Both in-memory and persistent DB rate limits fire, but the persistent DB check (3/hour by email) survives restart while the in-memory check does not, so behaviour is asymmetric. Legitimate users who submit 3 in a day then wait 1h may still see 429 depending on which layer trips first. Acceptable but worth documenting.

critical_code_review_comments: server.py has grown to 2964 lines — well past the 700-line guideline. Splitting into modules (auth.py, admin.py, posts.py, payments.py, security.py) would materially improve maintainability. Not blocking release but should be planned.

Rate-limiter is process-local (module-level dict _RATE_BUCKETS). If backend runs with >1 worker or is horizontally scaled, limits multiply by worker count. For a real-world release move this to Redis (INCR + EXPIRE) or at minimum document that backend must run single-worker.
Contact endpoint has TWO rate-limit layers (in-memory + DB count). If one changes the other is not kept in sync — consolidate to a single source of truth.

CORSMiddleware is registered AFTER app.include_router(api). FastAPI/Starlette allow this ordering but middleware added after routing can behave subtly differently on OPTIONS pre-flights. Move add_middleware BEFORE include_router as a best practice.

The `allow_credentials=cors_origins != '*'` toggle is correct but silent — if an operator sets CORS_ORIGINS=* (which is the current default when env var is missing), cookie-based auth breaks from the frontend and there is no log warning. Log a warning at startup when credentials are disabled.

/api/registered-birds calls rate_limit before Pydantic validation — this is defensive but means a user hitting rate-limit gets a Swedish 429 message while a user with a bad phone gets a different Swedish 400. Both fine, but the order should be documented as intentional.

The auto-created user path in POST /registered-birds (lines 675-720) generates a temp password and stores its hash, but the temp password itself is returned to the caller in the response body only when the response includes it — verify the frontend never persists this to console/localStorage.

_sanitize_post strips author_email and user_id from public output. Good. But author_name is still exposed and comes directly from `first_name last_name` of the poster. If a user puts their email in the last_name field during registration, it will leak on public posts. Consider validating first_name/last_name against an email-like regex during register.
test_credentials: Admin habib.nazary@hotmail.com / Jordgubbe234@u (from /app/memory/test_credentials.md). Fresh @example.se users are auto-generated per test.
seed_data_creation: Tests created ~4 fresh users (usera-*, userb-*, blocked-*, pwhash-*, sectest-*, rl-reg-*, rl-login-*), 1 pending bird (species=Wellensittich, ring=REG-<uuid>) with pending payment, ~10 birds with invalid phone (rejected at 400) that consumed the register-bird bucket, plus multiple posts (created+deleted by owner). Note: the register-bird rate-limit test leaves the IP 172.16.x.x bucket at 11/10 for 1h; subsequent tests use unique X-Forwarded-For addresses.
context_for_next_testing_agent: Rate-limit tests use unique X-Forwarded-For per test so they are independent. Backend was restarted once during this iteration (in-memory buckets are now populated for the specific IPs listed above but empty for fresh IPs). If you re-run test_security_iter16.py it will pass again because it always generates fresh users, fresh IPs and fresh test data. The contact test also uses fresh randomised IPs to avoid the persistent DB rate-limit layer. Stripe webhook tests use only signature verification — no real Stripe API is called. Admin block/unblock endpoints use PUT (not POST) — confirmed via inspection of /admin/users/{user_id}/block routes at server.py L1795, L1804.
rca_of_issues: One test initially failed (test_blocked_user_gets_403) because the block endpoint uses PUT and the test used POST — self-fixed. Two tests initially SKIPPED (bird comments and image-upload ownership) because there were no birds in the DB on gw1 before the rate-limit-test suite on gw0 created some; after backend restart and re-run all 26 passed.
